coaching.coaching: Plan how plan_review_phase bootstrap rule verification approach will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: plan_review_phase bootstrap rule verification approach

coaching.coaching: Run a quick pre-mortem on global store write lock held during N-ticket reduction: how could this plan fail?
coaching.subject: global store write lock held during N-ticket reduction

coaching.coaching: Weigh at least one structural alternative for pre-sign recheck scope and lock interaction overlap.
coaching.subject: pre-sign recheck scope and lock interaction overlap

coaching.subject: priority-floor vs blocking-floor terminology decision

coaching.subject: acceptance criteria completeness and terminology gaps

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact insertion position of `review-phase:`, `priority-floor:`, and `plan-material-pin:` lines within `build_manifest()`'s deterministic line order, and define corresponding parser functions (e.g., `manifest_review_phase`, `manifest_pins`) co-located with the manifest schema.
evidence: Plan: 'The signed manifest line review-phase: has exactly planning or execution. Execution reviews also carry priority-floor: 0.80.'
Plan: 'New plan reviews encode each pin as the signed manifest line plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>. Lines sort by role then canonical ID.'
build_manifest() in manifest.py emits a fixed-sequence list with no provision for review-phase, priority-floor, or plan-material-pin lines.
No manifest parser function (analogous to manifest_regver, manifest_deps, manifest_material) is defined for any of the three new line types.
findings.finding: The two new manifest lines `review-phase:` and `priority-floor:` are referenced throughout the plan but their insertion position within `build_manifest()`'s deterministic line order is never specified. The existing implementation has a fixed sequence (plan-review → ticket → material → model → runner → blocking → advisory → rebar-version → regver → disabled_builtins → refreshed-from → verified_at_sha → dep-lines). An executor must guess placement; signing and verification require byte-identical line order, so any divergence produces a permanently broken signature.
findings.location: Settled design → Signed plan-material pins; Settled design → Execution reviews and close gate; build_manifest() in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/manifest.py lines 190-224
scenarios: Executor adds review-phase line before rebar-version step; reader adds it after; HMAC-SHA256 verification fails on every previously-signed execution attestation.
Executor adds plan-material-pin lines before dep lines; drift-refresh path skips them because it only knows _DEP_PREFIX; pins are silently ignored by all existing readers.
findings.suggested_fix: Insert `review-phase:` immediately after `advisory:`, `priority-floor:` (when present) immediately after `review-phase:`, and `plan-material-pin:` lines (sorted by role then canonical ID) immediately before `dep` lines. Add `manifest_review_phase()`, `manifest_priority_floor()`, and `manifest_pins()` parser functions in manifest.py following the existing pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define precise, mutually exclusive criteria for 'unresolvable/unreadable' (causes all-or-nothing abort) vs. 'readable-but-model-indeterminate' (allows signing with indeterminate disposition), including treatment of archived, empty-material, and short-ID-only targets.
evidence: Plan: 'Pin collection runs before prerequisite LLM evaluation and is all-or-nothing: unresolved, malformed, duplicate, or unreadable required targets stop the review and prevent signing.'
Plan: 'The indeterminate prerequisite disposition exists only for a readable, successfully pinned target whose model evaluation or attribution cannot complete.'

The plan never defines what 'readable' means in this context (e.g., HTTP 200 with parseable material? Non-empty description? Non-archived?), nor what 'unresolvable' means precisely (short ID that cannot be canonicalized? Archived? Deleted?).

Plan separately mentions: 'A pinned target that has been deleted or is missing makes the pin stale' — suggesting deleted targets are handled post-signing, not at pin-collection time, further muddying the boundary.
findings.finding: The boundary between an 'unresolvable' prerequisite (which triggers all-or-nothing pin-collection failure and blocks signing) and a 'readable-but-indeterminate' prerequisite (which allows signing to proceed with an indeterminate disposition) is never formally specified. The plan uses both terms without defining what makes a target transition from one category to the other, leaving the fallback decision rule ambiguous.
findings.location: Settled design → Signed plan-material pins: 'unresolved, malformed, duplicate, or unreadable required targets stop the review'; 'The indeterminate prerequisite disposition exists only for a readable, successfully pinned target whose model evaluation or attribution cannot complete'
scenarios: Executor treats an archived ticket as 'readable' (it returns data) and proceeds to pin it; another executor treats it as 'unresolvable' because the plan says 'archived tickets remain readable' but also 'requires relationship repair'; signing behavior diverges.

A ticket with an empty description is 'readable' (store returns a record) but has no material to fingerprint; executor must decide: unresolvable (abort) or readable-indeterminate (proceed with empty fingerprint).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that verifies the bootstrap rule: pre-feature SNAPSHOTs with compiled status open/idea map to planning and all other statuses map to execution, and that the mapping takes effect on the first post-upgrade reduction.
evidence: 'For a pre-feature SNAPSHOT missing plan_review_phase, bootstrap planning when its compiled status is open or idea and execution for any other status.'
'A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction'
ABSENCE: no AC line names 'bootstrap', 'pre-feature SNAPSHOT', or 'version bump'
findings.finding: The plan describes a non-trivial bootstrap rule for pre-feature SNAPSHOTs ('bootstrap planning when its compiled status is open or idea and execution for any other status') and an associated reducer-cache version bump, but no acceptance criterion verifies this behavior. A wrong bootstrap mapping (e.g., an in-progress ticket bootstrapped as planning) would silently under-enforce the 0.80 floor on existing work.
findings.location: Settled design > Execution reviews and close gate (bootstrap paragraph); Acceptance Criteria (no covering line)
scenarios: An existing in_progress ticket has no plan_review_phase in its snapshot; bootstrap applies the wrong phase; the 0.80 floor is never enforced on close, silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Pre-feature SNAPSHOTs without plan_review_phase are bootstrapped to planning for open/idea status and execution for all other compiled statuses, effective on first post-upgrade reduction.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit acceptance criteria to 5967-4476-7f9f-442d for: (a) pre-feature snapshots without plan_review_phase bootstrap correctly to planning/execution based on compiled status, and (b) the reducer-cache version bump triggers the rule on first post-upgrade reduction.
evidence: Parent: 'For a pre-feature SNAPSHOT missing plan_review_phase, bootstrap planning when its compiled status is open or idea and execution for any other status. A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction; the derived value is then carried forward idempotently in later snapshots.'
5967 ACs list 15 items; none mentions pre-feature snapshot bootstrap, reducer-cache version bump, or backward-compatibility handling.
5967 AC4: 'Compaction snapshots preserve the projection.' — covers forward preservation only.
findings.finding: The parent's settled design specifies a bootstrap rule for pre-feature snapshots missing plan_review_phase: 'bootstrap planning when its compiled status is open or idea and execution for any other status. A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction.' No acceptance criterion in 5967-4476-7f9f-442d (the child that owns plan_review_phase) explicitly covers this bootstrap rule or the cache-version bump mechanism. The child's AC4 ('Compaction snapshots preserve the projection') is a forward-only property and does not verify backward-compatibility bootstrapping.
findings.location: child 5967-4476-7f9f-442d, Acceptance Criteria (missing bootstrap AC); parent Settled Design section 'Execution reviews and close gate'
scenarios: Post-upgrade reducer applies no bootstrap rule; existing in_progress tickets show planning phase incorrectly, allowing stale planning reviews to satisfy execution-mode gates.
Reducer-cache version is not bumped; first post-upgrade reduction uses old snapshot's missing field as if it were intentionally absent, causing indeterminate behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Add two ACs: 'Pre-feature snapshots without plan_review_phase bootstrap to planning when compiled status is open or idea, and execution otherwise.' and 'A reducer-cache version bump causes first post-upgrade reduction to apply the bootstrap rule idempotently.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a bound (cap on N pinned tickets, or time limit) for the lock-held pre-sign re-collection step, or justify why the existing lock-hold duration budget is sufficient for large graphs.
evidence: src/rebar/_store/lock.py: the write lock is a single store-global flock (fcntl + mkdir dual-leg); no per-ticket granularity
src/rebar/_commands/txn.py: existing lock-held operations are single-ticket appends, not multi-ticket reads
Plan text: 're-collect the complete relationship set plus every related fingerprint under the store write lock' — no bound on N tickets or lock-hold duration stated
ABSENCE: no stated cap on the number of pinned related tickets, no timeout/fallback for the lock-held re-collection phase
findings.finding: The pre-sign re-collection step requires reducing N related tickets (prerequisites + children) while holding the exclusive global store write lock. The existing lock is store-global and currently held only for single-ticket operations (a single append_event call). Holding it across N ticket reductions for large dependency graphs creates unbounded lock-hold latency with no stated cap, timeout, or fallback, risking starvation of concurrent writers.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins: 'Immediately before signing, re-collect the complete relationship set plus every related fingerprint under the store write lock'
scenarios: A ticket with 20+ prerequisites triggers a pin re-collection that reduces 20+ tickets while holding the exclusive lock, stalling every concurrent writer for seconds
A slow disk or large ticket causes one reduce_ticket call inside the lock to take 200ms; multiplied by N prerequisites, the lock is held for seconds
findings.suggested_fix: Add a cap (e.g., abort if N related tickets > configurable threshold, defaulting to a safe value like 50) or pre-fetch all related fingerprints outside the lock and re-verify only the fingerprints (not full reductions) under the lock—analogous to how the completion-verifier precheck runs the expensive LLM work outside the lock and only the STATUS append inside it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate one child (or add a shared orchestration step) to own a single unified pre-sign recollection that covers phase, material, AND relationship/fingerprints atomically under one store write lock, replacing the two independent rechecks in acaf and 5967.
evidence: acaf implementation: 'Immediately before signing, re-collect the full relationship set and fingerprints under the store write lock, compare against the pre-LLM PlanRelationSnapshot, and abort on any difference.'
5967 implementation: 'Snapshot lifecycle phase and material before review, then recheck both immediately before signing so concurrent transitions or edits cannot certify the wrong mode.'
Parent settled design: 'Review snapshots phase, material, and pins before the LLM call and rechecks them before signing.' — singular 'rechecks' suggests one atomic operation.
No child explicitly describes or owns a unified pre-sign recheck covering phase + material + relationship/fingerprint under a single write lock.
findings.finding: acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c and 5967-4476-7f9f-442d each describe an independent pre-sign recheck: acaf rechecks the full relationship set and all fingerprints under the store write lock; 5967 rechecks lifecycle phase and material before signing. No child explicitly owns a single unified atomic recheck that covers all three dimensions (phase, material, and relationship/fingerprint) under one store write lock. The parent says 'Review snapshots phase, material, and pins before the LLM call and rechecks them before signing' — implying a single unified recollection. If implemented as two separate lock acquisitions, there is a race window between the two rechecks.
findings.location: child acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c (pre-sign re-collection) and child 5967-4476-7f9f-442d (sign-time phase recheck)
scenarios: Phase recheck (5967) succeeds; relationship changes occur before fingerprint recheck (acaf); fingerprint recheck succeeds because the new related ticket has identical narrow material. A pin is signed for wrong relationships because the two rechecks were not atomic.

A concurrent open event between the two recheck calls resets phase to planning after 5967's recheck passes but before acaf's lock, resulting in an execution-phase attestation on a planning-mode ticket.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC12's terminology with the manifest field name: replace 'blocking floor' with 'priority-floor' (or define 'blocking floor' as an alias for the priority-floor manifest field) so the criterion unambiguously references the signed artifact it verifies.
evidence: 'Execution reviews also carry priority-floor: 0.80'
AC12: 'Execution reviews apply the fixed 0.80 blocking floor'
findings.finding: The manifest field is named 'priority-floor' in the plan body but the acceptance criterion (AC12) calls it the 'blocking floor,' creating a terminology mismatch between the specified manifest artifact and its verifying criterion.
findings.location: Settled design > Execution reviews and close gate ('priority-floor: 0.80'); AC12 ('Execution reviews apply the fixed 0.80 blocking floor')
scenarios: A reviewer or implementer checking AC12 against the manifest format may not recognize 'blocking floor' as referring to the 'priority-floor' manifest line, leading to ambiguity in what the criterion is actually verifying.
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC12 to: 'Execution reviews apply the fixed priority-floor of 0.80 as the blocking threshold for all blocking criteria.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether transient read failures during pin collection (e.g., OSError) should be treated as abort-with-retry (indeterminate, retriable) or abort-with-repair (same as permanently missing), and surface a distinct error code/reason to the operator.
evidence: Plan text: 'A pinned target that has been deleted or is missing makes the pin stale and requires relationship repair followed by re-review' — this is the permanent-missing remediation
Plan text: 'unresolved, malformed, duplicate, or unreadable required targets stop the review' — transient unreadable is lumped with permanent missing
ABSENCE: no retry or transient-failure distinction for pin collection errors
findings.finding: The all-or-nothing pin collection rule ('unresolved, malformed, duplicate, or unreadable required targets stop the review') does not distinguish between a transiently unreadable target (e.g., a file-system race during re-collection) and a permanently deleted/missing one. Both are treated as abort, but the plan only specifies a remediation path ('relationship repair followed by re-review') for the permanently-missing case. A transient read failure during pin collection—or during the pre-sign re-collection under the write lock—would abort a completed LLM review with no retry signal, wasting the LLM cost.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins: 'unresolved, malformed, duplicate, or unreadable required targets stop the review and prevent signing'
scenarios: A prerequisite ticket's cache file is being atomically rewritten by a concurrent compaction when pin collection reads it; the read fails transiently; the entire review (including completed LLM passes) is aborted
Pre-sign re-collection under the write lock reads a prerequisite that another writer just modified; if the read fails mid-lock, the review aborts with no clear operator guidance

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the store-wide cache invalidation caused by the reducer-cache version bump and either accept it with a note to operators, or specify a lower-impact activation mechanism (e.g., project the phase lazily on first STATUS event post-upgrade without a global cache bump).
evidence: src/rebar/reducer/_cache.py: _REDUCER_CACHE_VERSION = 6; bumping it causes every .cache.json to be invalidated (the version is folded into the dir hash, so all prior caches miss)
ABSENCE: no mention of the operational impact of a store-wide cache invalidation or mitigation strategy
findings.finding: The reducer-cache version bump required to activate the `plan_review_phase` bootstrap rule is a store-wide cold-cache event: every ticket's `.cache.json` is invalidated on first access post-upgrade. The plan does not acknowledge this operational impact (high first-read latency spike across all tickets after deploy) or provide a rollout mitigation (e.g., warm the cache in the background after upgrade, or stagger the bump).
findings.location: Settled design / Execution reviews and close gate: 'A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction'
scenarios: A large rebar deployment with thousands of tickets sees a latency spike on the first request after upgrade as every ticket re-reduces from scratch
An agent reads multiple tickets immediately post-upgrade; each ticket-read incurs full reduction instead of cache hit

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the gold-baseline fixture suite pre-exists (and where) or must be authored as part of this work, and enumerate the minimum required scenario categories (e.g., single-prerequisite, multi-prerequisite within headroom, tail item that forces a smaller bin) so the fidelity AC is fully measurable.
evidence: 'Packed behavior uses the existing whole-child bin-packing pattern and is release-gated against a one-prerequisite-per-call gold baseline: packed recall must be no lower, packed false acceptance no higher, every prerequisite must receive a record, and attribution errors must be zero. Tail-item fixtures are mandatory.'
ABSENCE: no statement of whether the gold baseline test suite pre-exists, no definition of what scenarios constitute tail-item fixtures, no minimum corpus size.
findings.finding: The fidelity contract for packed prerequisite evaluation references a 'one-prerequisite-per-call gold baseline' and mandates 'tail-item fixtures', but the plan does not specify whether these fixtures already exist or must be authored as part of this work, nor does it define the minimum scenario set (number of prerequisites, tail-item characteristics) that constitutes an adequate baseline. An executor cannot tell whether to locate existing fixtures or create them, and the AC cannot be verified without a known, agreed corpus.
findings.location: Settled design → Prerequisite consistency review (last paragraph); Acceptance Criteria line 'Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract.'
scenarios: An executor implementing packed evaluation creates fixtures that cover only happy-path batches; tail-edge behavior goes untested and the AC is declared passing without real coverage.
An executor spends time searching for a non-existent pre-built fixture suite rather than authoring one, or vice versa.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-bullet under 'Prerequisite consistency review': 'The gold-baseline fixture suite is created as part of this work and lives in tests/prerequisite_packing/. It must include: (a) a single-prerequisite case, (b) a multi-prerequisite case that fits within one LLM call, (c) a tail-item case where the last prerequisite alone exceeds remaining context and forces a one-per-call fallback. Recall and false-acceptance are measured over this fixed set.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion specifying that a pin referencing a deleted or missing ticket marks the attestation as having a stale pin, and that the condition requires relationship repair and re-review before the close gate passes under enforcement.
evidence: 'A pinned target that has been deleted or is missing makes the pin stale and requires relationship repair followed by re-review.'
ABSENCE: no AC line mentions deleted targets, stale pins, or the repair requirement
findings.finding: The plan specifies that a deleted or missing pinned target 'makes the pin stale and requires relationship repair followed by re-review,' but no acceptance criterion covers this state or its required resolution path. This is a described failure mode with operational consequences and no verifiable contract.
findings.location: Settled design > Signed plan-material pins (last bullet); Acceptance Criteria (no covering line)
scenarios: A prerequisite ticket is archived after a downstream review is signed; the attestation contains a stale pin; enforcement is on; the downstream close gate behavior is undefined by the AC contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the signing/verification mechanism (e.g., sign each line independently, define a canonical subset, or use an append-only section) that allows pre-upgrade readers to verify new manifests without digest mismatch, and add this to the rollback plan.
evidence: 'Older readers ignore additive pin lines without invalidating the signature'
'Pins are automatic signed attestation metadata'
No description of a signing scheme (e.g., sign-what-you-know, append-only structure, or manifest sections) that permits partial-line digest verification
No rollback path for old readers that encounter new-format signed manifests
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'older readers ignore additive pin lines without invalidating the signature' but provides no mechanism by which signature verification can succeed when the signed manifest now contains lines (plan-material-pin, review-phase, priority-floor) that old code neither emits nor includes in its digest computation. If the signature covers the full manifest text, old readers recomputing the digest over only the lines they understand will see a mismatch and treat a valid new attestation as corrupt — an unacknowledged breakage with no rollback path for old-code readers.
findings.location: Settled design > Enforcement and rollout: 'Pin lines are additive manifest metadata: readers predating this feature ignore unknown lines and therefore treat the attestation like a legacy unpinned review.'
scenarios: An old agent or reader verifies a new-format attestation: it skips unknown lines but computes digest over fewer lines than were signed, causing spurious signature failure and treating valid work as unreviewed.
A code rollback after new attestations have been written leaves old code unable to verify any attestation signed under the new scheme.
findings.suggested_fix: Define that the signature covers only the canonically ordered set of known-stable manifest fields, and that new additive lines (pin, review-phase, priority-floor) are recorded in a clearly delimited extension block excluded from the legacy digest — or adopt a two-digest approach where the legacy digest is preserved verbatim and a separate extended digest covers all lines.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the rollback plan to address snapshot/compaction compatibility: either state that old reducers safely ignore unknown snapshot fields and fall back to bootstrap logic, or provide a compaction-drain/re-compact procedure for rollback scenarios.
evidence: 'A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction; the derived value is then carried forward idempotently in later snapshots'
'Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback; no stored attestation must be rewritten'
No statement about forward/backward compatibility of compiled-state snapshots containing plan_review_phase
No compaction-drain or snapshot-rewrite step for rollback
findings.finding: The reducer-cache version bump that bootstraps plan_review_phase into compaction snapshots is not covered by the stated rollback plan. Disabling enforcement flags is the only rollback step named, but once new code has written snapshots containing plan_review_phase, rolling back the code leaves old reducers encountering an unknown projection field in stored snapshots — the plan does not describe whether old code ignores unknown snapshot fields, how to drain or re-compact snapshots on rollback, or any other reversibility mechanism for this schema change.
findings.location: Settled design > Execution reviews and close gate: 'A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction'; Settled design > Enforcement and rollout: 'Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback'
scenarios: Operator rolls back code after new snapshots have been written with plan_review_phase; old reducer encounters the unknown field and either ignores it (silent wrong bootstrap) or fails (crash/undefined behavior).
A partial rollout across multiple nodes means some nodes write new-format snapshots while rolled-back nodes read them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit documentation scope statements for verify.enforce_plan_material_pins, verify.require_plan_review_for_close, review-phase, and priority-floor manifest lines — at minimum a reference to the advanced attestation/gate documentation alongside the existing plan-material-pin callout.
evidence: 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins defaults to false'
'verify.require_plan_review_for_close defaults to false'
'The signed manifest line review-phase: has exactly planning or execution'
'priority-floor: 0.80'
'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation' — no parallel statement for the other two config flags or the review-phase/priority-floor line formats
findings.finding: The two new user-facing configuration flags (verify.enforce_plan_material_pins and verify.require_plan_review_for_close) and the new manifest line formats (review-phase, priority-floor) are not explicitly earmarked for documentation, even though plan-material-pin is. These flags directly control gate behavior that operators must understand to enable safely, and the manifest formats are protocol contracts consumed by multiple surfaces.
findings.location: Settled design > Enforcement and rollout; Settled design > Execution reviews and close gate; Health and documentation: 'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation'
scenarios: An operator enabling verify.require_plan_review_for_close finds no documentation explaining the compatible-phase requirement or the close-profile exemptions.
A tooling author implementing a manifest parser has no documented spec for review-phase or priority-floor lines.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Health and documentation section, extend the existing sentence to: 'plan-material-pin, review-phase, priority-floor, verify.enforce_plan_material_pins, and verify.require_plan_review_for_close are documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurement step (e.g., retrospective scoring of a sample of historical in-progress ticket reviews against the 0.80 floor, or a pilot with metrics) to empirically validate that 0.80 is an appropriate execution-phase risk threshold before hardcoding it in the protocol.
evidence: "The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config."
No spike, benchmark, or historical-review-score measurement is planned to validate that 0.80 separates risky from acceptable execution reviews in practice.
AC: 'Execution reviews apply the fixed 0.80 blocking floor.' — no pass/fail measurement criteria for the threshold value itself.
findings.finding: The 0.80 execution blocking floor is explicitly acknowledged as 'not an empirically claimed optimum' and is asserted with no measurement plan, spike, or pilot to validate it is appropriately calibrated for its stated purpose (risk policy for in-progress work).
findings.location: Settled design / Execution reviews and close gate — 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
scenarios: 0.80 is too high: legitimate execution reviews are routinely blocked, forcing unnecessary re-reviews and slowing delivery.
0.80 is too low: the floor provides no meaningful additional protection over normal thresholds, making the execution-phase gate theater.
Future policy change requires a code change with no data to guide what the new value should be.
findings.suggested_fix: Run a retrospective: apply the 0.80 floor to a representative set of completed in-progress tickets and measure how many would have been blocked vs. passed, then compare outcomes to confirm the threshold is well-calibrated. State success criteria (e.g., <X% false blocks on known-good reviews). This does not require changing the threshold to be configurable — just provide the empirical basis before committing to the value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit documentation task to create or update an ADR (or AGENTS.md / design doc) covering the plan-material-pin manifest contract, plan_review_phase projection lifecycle, the pre-sign write-lock re-collection protocol, and the prerequisite consistency review pipeline stage.
evidence: The 'Health and documentation' section mentions only user-facing advanced attestation/gate docs for plan-material-pin; no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or internal design doc is mentioned anywhere in the plan.

Four distinct novel architectural patterns are introduced: signed manifest pin lines, a new reducer projection, a new specialized-criterion pipeline stage, and a write-lock re-collection protocol — each a decision future maintainers need context for.
ABSENCE: no 'ADR', 'AGENTS.md', 'design doc', or 'architecture decision' appears anywhere in the plan text.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple novel architectural decisions — the plan-material-pin manifest line format, the plan_review_phase reducer projection, the specialized prerequisite consistency review pipeline, and the pre-sign re-collection-under-lock pattern — without specifying any ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update to capture these decisions.
findings.location: Settled design (all subsections); Health and documentation section — 'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation.'
scenarios: A future engineer modifying the signing flow doesn't know why pre-sign re-collection exists under the write lock (vs. optimistic check) and removes it as seemingly redundant.
The prerequisite consistency review pipeline's bin-packing gate rationale is lost; a maintainer relaxes or removes the gold-baseline gate not knowing it was a correctness contract.
The plan_review_phase bootstrap rule for legacy SNAPSHOTs is undocumented architecturally, leading to conflicting reimplementations during compaction refactors.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that compiled-state and compaction snapshots containing `plan_review_phase` are safe for old code to read post-rollback (e.g., old reducers treat unknown snapshot fields as absent/ignored, bootstrapping the legacy default), or add a snapshot-version migration step to the rollback procedure.
evidence: 'A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction; the derived value is then carried forward idempotently in later snapshots.'
'Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback; no stored attestation must be rewritten.'
'Older readers ignore additive pin lines' — specified for manifest lines but NOT stated for compiled-state / compaction snapshot fields containing plan_review_phase.
Absence: no statement that old code safely ignores or handles the new `plan_review_phase` key in snapshots it reads post-rollback.
findings.finding: The plan specifies rollback as 'disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback; no stored attestation must be rewritten,' and separately specifies that manifest pin lines are ignored by older readers. However, it does not address backward compatibility of compiled-state and compaction snapshots that now carry the new `plan_review_phase` field. After a reducer-cache version bump fires and snapshots are written with `plan_review_phase`, a code rollback leaves old code reading new snapshots containing an unknown field — the plan does not specify whether old code handles this gracefully (ignores it, errors, or silently derives wrong phase).
findings.location: Settled design › Execution reviews and close gate — 'A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction'; Enforcement and rollout — 'Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback'
scenarios: A team upgrades rebar, triggers a re-reduction (version bump fires), and compaction snapshots are written with `plan_review_phase`. A rollback to the prior code version then occurs. Old reducer code reads snapshots with the unknown field — depending on the deserialization strategy, this could produce a parse error halting reduction, or silently omit the field and bootstrap an incorrect phase, causing wrong close-gate or review-compatibility decisions without any error message.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a statement to the rollback section: 'Compiled-state snapshots written after the version bump carry `plan_review_phase`; reducers treat unknown fields as absent and bootstrap the default (planning for open/idea, execution otherwise), so a code rollback without snapshot cleanup is safe. Alternatively, compaction snapshots are regenerated on demand, so a rollback that also clears the reducer cache produces correct legacy behavior.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate whether plan-material pins should be encoded as a structured in-toto predicate field (extending the existing `rebar.opcert.v1` Statement) rather than opaque manifest lines, given that the codebase already uses in-toto v1 Statement format for op-cert subjects — and document the justification for the chosen approach.
evidence: src/rebar/attest/opcert.py lines 24-25: 'Subject binds {ticket id, material fingerprint, merged-log commit} in an in-toto v1 Statement'
src/rebar/attest/opcert.py opcert_subject_digest() function builds an in-toto v1 Statement for the existing op-cert signing
Plan phrase: 'Signed pins extend the existing computed-on-read attestation-validity model: the downstream review cryptographically records exactly what related plan material it relied on'
ABSENCE: no discussion of in-toto link attestations, SLSA dependency provenance, or why manifest-line embedding is preferred over extending the existing in-toto Statement predicate
in-toto framework's link metadata concept is a direct prior-art match for cross-step input/output hash pinning
findings.finding: The plan introduces plan-material-pin manifest lines as a custom cross-ticket dependency hash-pinning scheme, yet the codebase already uses in-toto v1 Statement format as its op-cert signing substrate (opcert.py implements in-toto subject/predicate/digest). The in-toto framework has a well-established answer for exactly this problem: *link attestations* record signed input/output artifact fingerprints per step, and a *layout* verifies the chain — which is structurally identical to what signed pins accomplish. The plan does not acknowledge this prior art, does not explain why manifest-line embedding is preferred over extending the existing in-toto Statement predicate (e.g., adding a 'related-material-pins' field to the predicate payload), and does not evaluate whether structured in-toto links would provide stronger verifiability guarantees (e.g., machine-parseable by standard in-toto verifiers). Without this justification, the plan may be reinventing a solved problem on top of the very substrate that already solves it.
findings.location: Settled design > Signed plan-material pins: 'New plan reviews encode each pin as the signed manifest line plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>'
scenarios: The plan ships a custom manifest-line pin format when an in-toto predicate extension would achieve the same goal with structured, machine-verifiable, standard-compliant attestation metadata that third-party tooling could consume.
A future attempt to integrate with SLSA tooling finds the pin data locked in a bespoke manifest-line format rather than accessible via the existing in-toto Statement predicate, requiring a migration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the exact post-rollback schema state: confirm that rolled-back (older) reducer code safely ignores the `plan_review_phase` field in snapshots it reads, and that old attestation verifiers safely ignore `plan-material-pin:`, `review-phase:`, and `priority-floor:` manifest lines — or specify a compensating-forward procedure if they do not.
evidence: 'Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback; no stored attestation must be rewritten.'
'A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction; the derived value is then carried forward idempotently in later snapshots.'
Plan does NOT state: 'old reducers ignore unknown snapshot fields' or 'post-rollback, compiled states with plan_review_phase are safe for the prior code version.'
POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE criterion: plan must assert schema state after rollback, not merely that rollback procedure exits.
findings.finding: The plan describes rollback as 'disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback; no stored attestation must be rewritten,' but it does not assert the schema STATE that old code encounters after rollback. Specifically: (1) compiled-state snapshots that have been written with the new `plan_review_phase` field will be read by the rolled-back (older) code — the plan never confirms that old reducers tolerate unknown snapshot fields. (2) Attestation manifests written with new lines (`plan-material-pin:`, `review-phase:`, `priority-floor:`) are claimed forward-compatible for 'readers predating this feature,' but this claim is made in the context of gradual rollout, not explicit post-rollback verification. If the old reducer fails or panics on an unknown snapshot field, rollback leaves the system in a broken state despite 'exiting 0.'
findings.location: Settled design → Enforcement and rollout (rollback paragraph); Settled design → Execution reviews (plan_review_phase bootstrap paragraph)
scenarios: After partial rollout, some tickets have snapshots with plan_review_phase embedded; code is rolled back; old reducer encounters unknown field and throws, leaving those tickets unreducible until manually repaired.

After rollback, manifest lines written by the new code are encountered by old verification logic that does not have the 'ignore unknown lines' contract — if that contract is not enforced in the old binary, attestations become unreadable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a paragraph under 'Enforcement and rollout' that explicitly states: (a) the snapshot serialization format uses unknown-field-tolerant deserialization (e.g., serde `#[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]` is NOT used, or equivalent), so old reducers silently drop `plan_review_phase`; and (b) attestation manifest parsing in old readers already uses a 'skip unknown lines' contract (cite the existing parser). If either is not already guaranteed, add a pre-rollout compatibility test fixture: a snapshot/manifest with new fields parsed by the old code must not error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `PlanRelationSnapshot` as a concrete data structure with named fields (at minimum: set of (role, canonical_id) pairs, mapping of canonical_id → material_fingerprint), equality semantics, and the exact store operations used to collect it.
evidence: Plan names 'PlanRelationSnapshot' as a specific named data structure.
No definition of PlanRelationSnapshot fields, type, or equality semantics appears anywhere in the plan.
Search for 'PlanRelationSnapshot' in the codebase returns no results — it does not yet exist.
findings.finding: The `PlanRelationSnapshot` struct — used to capture the pre-LLM relationship+fingerprint state and then compared under write lock before signing — is referenced by name but never defined. Its fields (which relationships, which fingerprints, in what format) are unspecified. An executor cannot implement the 'compare PlanRelationSnapshot before signing' abort protocol without knowing what the snapshot contains.
findings.location: Settled design → Signed plan-material pins: 're-collect the complete relationship set plus every related fingerprint under the store write lock and compare it with the pre-LLM PlanRelationSnapshot'
scenarios: Executor includes only direct-child IDs in the snapshot but not fingerprints; a concurrent fingerprint change goes undetected; a stale pin is signed.
Executor includes relationship direction metadata in the snapshot; reader re-collects without it; snapshots never compare equal; no review can sign successfully.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a formal schema (JSON schema or equivalent) for the prerequisite consistency coverage record, specifying all fields of `{prerequisite_id, disposition, findings}` and the shape of each finding entry within the record, co-located with the prerequisite criterion specification.
evidence: Plan specifies three disposition values: consistent, finding, indeterminate.
Plan specifies 'consistent requires an empty findings list; finding requires one or more candidate findings all attributed to that same prerequisite_id'.
No JSON schema, dataclass, or TypedDict definition is provided for the coverage record.
Pass 2 'verifies substance and prerequisite_id attribution' and 'only an upheld finding record can proceed to normal Pass 3' — both require a concrete schema to implement.
findings.finding: The prerequisite consistency review output schema `{prerequisite_id, disposition, findings}` is new and distinct from the existing plan-review finding schema, but it is never formally specified: field types, required/optional status, the shape of `findings` when `disposition=consistent` (must be empty list per plan text) vs. `disposition=finding`, and the full field set of each finding entry within the record are all absent. Pass 2 and Pass 3 promotion logic depend on this schema.
findings.location: Settled design → Prerequisite consistency review: 'Pass 1 returns exactly one coverage record per canonical prerequisite: {prerequisite_id, disposition, findings}'
scenarios: Executor emits findings as plain strings; Pass 2 verifier expects dicts with 'finding', 'criteria', 'prerequisite_id' fields; attribution check crashes.
Executor omits prerequisite_id from individual findings inside the record; Pass 3 promotion path cannot verify attribution, silently promotes unattributed findings.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'derived health contract' as a concrete schema (fields, enum values, derivation rules from attestation state) that all five named consumer surfaces (CLI show, MCP detail, audit, close errors, web UI) must conform to.
evidence: Plan: 'Derived pin and review-phase health appears in detailed CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, and the web ticket-review UI.'
Plan: 'When enforcement is off, the UI labels drift advisory.'
No field names, schema, or enum vocabulary for the health object is provided anywhere in the plan.
Search for 'derived health contract' finds only the two plan-review cache JSON files referencing it as a gap — it does not exist in the codebase.
findings.finding: The 'derived health contract' is referenced in AC #27 and the Health section as a shared interface that all detailed surfaces (CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, web ticket-review UI) must consume, but it is never defined. The field names, enum values (e.g. 'advisory' vs. 'drift' label for disabled enforcement), and the structural shape of the health object are absent.
findings.location: Settled design → Health and documentation: 'All detailed review surfaces consume the shared derived-health contract'; AC #27: 'All detailed review surfaces consume the shared derived-health contract'
scenarios: CLI show team implements `{pin_drift: bool, phase: str}`; web UI team implements `{drift_status: 'advisory'|'invalid', review_phase: str}`; the two diverge, AC #27 cannot be verified.
Audit output omits a field the close-gate error message depends on; close errors display nothing for pin drift.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the gold baseline corpus (minimum fixture count, source, tail-item definition), measurement protocol (how recall and false-acceptance are computed), and the pointer to the existing whole-child bin-packing pattern the prerequisite criterion should mirror.
evidence: Plan: 'packed recall must be no lower, packed false acceptance no higher, every prerequisite must receive a record, and attribution errors must be zero. Tail-item fixtures are mandatory. Failure keeps the smaller-bin or one-per-call fallback rather than shipping degraded packing.'
No corpus definition, fixture format, measurement harness, or sample size is specified.
The 'whole-child bin-packing pattern' referenced as the model exists for child criteria but no pointer to that specification is provided.
findings.finding: The packed prerequisite bin-packing 'gold baseline' release gate references specific fidelity metrics (packed recall ≥ baseline, false acceptance ≤ baseline, attribution errors = 0, tail-item fixtures mandatory) but never specifies what corpus constitutes the baseline, how it is collected, or the measurement protocol. This is a processing protocol for a release decision that is referenced but not co-located with the specification an executor needs to implement the gate.
findings.location: Settled design → Prerequisite consistency review: 'Packed behavior uses the existing whole-child bin-packing pattern and is release-gated against a one-prerequisite-per-call gold baseline'
scenarios: Executor measures only recall on a hand-picked 3-prerequisite fixture; ships packed evaluation that degrades on real 10-prerequisite tickets; release gate was meaningless.
Two implementors disagree on whether 'attribution errors = 0' means zero across the corpus or zero per-call; the gate passes one and fails the other.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decompose 75de AC1 into per-consumer acceptance criteria (claim, close, attestation reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit, derived health) or add named-consumer test requirements so each path is individually verifiable.
evidence: Parent AC7: 'Enabled pin enforcement invalidates every named consumer after related material drift.'
Parent settled design: 'Pin validity uses one implementation wherever an attestation is already being evaluated: claim, close, attestation reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit, and derived health.'
75de AC1: 'Pin-aware attestations become invalid on actual related material drift in every named lifecycle consumer when enforcement is enabled.' — one AC, no per-consumer decomposition.
75de verification: 'Run focused attestation invalidation, reuse, drift-refresh, re-sign, and config tests.' — verification steps name consumers but ACs do not.
findings.finding: Parent AC7 ('Enabled pin enforcement invalidates every named consumer after related material drift') is carried entirely by 75de-02b0-d454-410b AC1, which states 'every named lifecycle consumer' as a single undecomposed assertion. The parent names seven distinct consumers (claim, close, attestation reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit, derived health), but no child acceptance criterion verifies each one individually. This is a partial-without-remainder pattern: if any one consumer (e.g., re-sign or drift-refresh) is missed in implementation, the single aggregate AC cannot be falsified at review time.
findings.location: child 75de-02b0-d454-410b, Acceptance Criteria AC1
scenarios: Re-sign path omits pin check; AC1 passes because claim, close, and reuse were tested.
Drift-refresh path is skipped; unified AC1 still ticks as met by the other consumers' tests.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace single AC1 with: 'Pin-aware attestation invalidation is verified for each of: claim, close, attestation reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and derived health when enforcement is enabled.' Either as seven sub-bullets or as a dedicated list within the verification section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit intra-file ownership boundaries for attest.py across acaf, 75de, and 5967 (e.g., which functions each child adds vs. extends), or consolidate the sign-time recheck into one child's scope.
evidence: acaf file_impact: 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py — Collect and bind related-ticket material during signing.'
75de file_impact: 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py — Compute structured pin health and validity.'
5967 file_impact: 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py — Enforce phase compatibility and sign-time rechecks.'
All three describe different concerns (collection vs. health vs. phase) in the same file without naming mutually exclusive function boundaries.
findings.finding: Three children — acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c (pin collection), 75de-02b0-d454-410b (pin validity/health derivation), and 5967-4476-7f9f-442d (phase snapshot and sign-time recheck) — all list src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py in their file_impact for distinct but interconnected concerns. No child specifies which sections or functions it owns within that file, and no explicit integration boundary is described. While sequential ordering (acaf→75de→5967) mitigates merge conflicts, it leaves the responsibility division in attest.py implicit, risking overlapping rewrites or missed integration during implementation hand-offs.
findings.location: child acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c, child 75de-02b0-d454-410b, child 5967-4476-7f9f-442d — all file_impact: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py
scenarios: 5967's sign-time recheck implementation partially re-implements 75de's validity check, creating two enforcement paths rather than calling the shared profile.
acaf's pin-collection function is refactored by 75de for health derivation in a way that breaks 5967's recheck contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Amend 5967-4476-7f9f-442d AC3 to explicitly include force transitions: 'Force transitions follow the same target-status rule (in_progress sets execution, open sets planning); other force targets preserve the projection.'
evidence: Parent settled design: 'Force transitions follow the same target-status rule' and 'other targets preserve the value, including force transitions.'
5967 AC3: 'Other status transitions preserve the projection.' — no mention of force transitions.

Force transitions are a distinct code path in transition.py; the omission from the AC scope allows an implementation that handles normal transitions correctly but silently skips phase updates for force transitions.
findings.finding: 5967-4476-7f9f-442d AC3 states 'Other status transitions preserve the projection' but does not explicitly include force transitions. The parent's settled design states 'Force transitions follow the same target-status rule' and 'other targets preserve the value, including force transitions.' This is a scope-narrowing gap: the child's AC could be satisfied by testing only normal status transitions while leaving the force-transition code path unverified.
findings.location: child 5967-4476-7f9f-442d, Acceptance Criteria AC3
scenarios: Force-close to in_progress via force transition does not set execution phase; later review incorrectly treats the ticket as planning-mode; an execution-phase review is rejected even though the ticket is actively in progress.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC3 to: 'Force transitions follow the same target-status rule; other status transitions (normal and force) preserve the projection.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 that the integration test is a consumer-side contract test against acaf's PlanRelationSnapshot interface, and that acaf owns backward-compatibility of that contract; or assign joint ownership of the test fixture explicitly.
evidence: 7c27 implementation: 'Consume the pin story's PlanRelationSnapshot contract; use it only to construct isolated specialized-criterion inputs. Never inject prerequisite material into general finder/verifier calls. Add an integration test proving pin collection plus criterion routing share one preload without prompt leakage.'
acaf file_impact: owns PlanRelationSnapshot construction in orchestrator.py.
7c27 has no explicit provision for handling failures caused by acaf's preload contract changes.
findings.finding: 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 specifies an integration test ('proving pin collection plus criterion routing share one preload without prompt leakage') that spans two children's implementation scope: acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c owns the PlanRelationSnapshot preload, and 7c27 owns the criterion routing. The test is assigned to 7c27, but its correctness depends on acaf's preload implementation. No ownership handoff or shared test fixture is described, leaving it unclear who is responsible if the test fails due to a defect in acaf's preload rather than 7c27's routing.
findings.location: child 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56, Implementation section ('Add an integration test proving pin collection plus criterion routing share one preload without prompt leakage')
scenarios: acaf changes the PlanRelationSnapshot structure after 7c27's integration test is written; the test fails but 7c27's implementation is correct; no child owns the fix because the contract change is in acaf.

findings.finding: An unresolvable prerequisite ticket triggers two contradictory outcomes across sections: pin collection (all-or-nothing) prevents signing entirely, while the prerequisite consistency review guarantees that an indeterminate record 'cannot block'. If both mechanisms apply to the same unresolvable ticket, an implementer faces contradictory direction — is signing blocked (pin collection rule) or is the outcome merely indeterminate and non-blocking (prerequisite review rule)?
findings.location: Settled design — 'Signed plan-material pins' section vs. 'Prerequisite consistency review' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Signed plan-material pins' section: 'Pin collection is attempted before LLM evaluation; an unresolvable target causes signing to fail regardless of the prerequisite consistency review outcome. The indeterminate disposition applies only when the prerequisite ticket is readable but LLM evaluation or attribution cannot be completed.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
